title: Chapter 22: The One Who Lights the Light
paragraphs: Lord Valtor's ice spears tore through the flesh of the void with mathematical, ruthless precision. A legion of the dead, knowing neither fatigue nor fear, formed an impenetrable wall of shields from old, rusted bones. They held the flanks, giving the refugees air to breathe. Dzhanavar and Rene fought in the center, their movements merging into a single, desperate rhythm of survival.
It seemed this absurd union of outcasts had managed to do the impossible—they had stopped the end of the world at the very gates of Agdash.
But the rotting heavens did not know how to lose.

A muffled, visceral crack rolled across the firmament, from which the marble walls of the city cracked with deep fissures. The portal vortex, which had previously only spewed Reapers, suddenly expanded three times, pouring out concentrated gloom.

From the torn fabric of the universe, slowly, clinging with bony growths to the edges of reality, crawled the Behemoth. This was no ordinary monster. This was necrosis itself, having found physical form. A creature the size of a fortress tower, whose body consisted of intertwined gray limbs, spikes, and pulsing ulcers.

When the Behemoth landed heavily on the ashen road, the earth shook so much that half of Valtor's skeletons simply crumbled to dust. The shockwave threw Rene and Dzhanavar dozens of paces back. Kirah, who tried to bar the monster's path, was swept away by one careless swipe of a colossal paw. The giant demon crashed into the crowd of refugees, knocking over old carts and choking on his own blood.
The Behemoth towered over the hydrols and gnomes cowering at the gates. It slowly raised its thousand-ton, blade-studded limb, preparing to wipe them into dust with one strike.
Lord Valtor raised a hand, sending a swarm of ice blades at the monster, but the vampire's ancient magic simply evaporated before reaching the gray armor. The Behemoth was too great. Too absolute.
Yashil, clutching a bent iron pipe, stood right under the descending shadow of death. He did not look away. He was ready.
But at that moment, the space between the goblin and the monster tore with a piercing, ringing whistle.
From above, directly from the jade parapet of Agdash, a black meteor fell.
Avotu.

The last apprentice of Sivarus landed with perfect, frightening grace. The Blade of Nature in his hand did not emit a chaotic, destructive howl as before. Now the sword sang. Purely, steadily, and deathly. And the black flame above the elf's pale face no longer absorbed light—at its roots, golden sparks burned brightly, indomitably.
Avotu did not wait for the Behemoth's strike. He became the strike himself.

The elf vanished in a flash of black-gold fire and appeared directly above the monster's colossal paw. One blurred, geometrically flawless stroke of the sword—and a condensed squall of air severed the giant limb with a wet crunch. The Behemoth let out a deafening screech, reeling back. Acid blood poured onto the stones in a waterfall.
Avotu landed in front of Yashil, shielding the goblin and the crowd of refugees. His broad, bruised-steel-clad shoulders rose heavily, but in his posture was not a trace of the former doom.
"You promised to be a shield, traveler," Yashil said quietly through the din of battle, and a faint smile flickered on his blood-stained lips.
"And I will keep my oath, goblin," Avotu replied without turning. His voice was steady as basalt.

The Behemoth of the Void, roaring in pain, collapsed all its unimaginable power upon the elf. A dance began, watched with bated breath even by the High Elves from the walls of Agdash. Avotu moved at the limit of his race's physical and magical capabilities. He parried blows capable of crushing mountains, deflected them with sliding blocks, striking cascades of sparks, and dealt compensatory, precise wounds.

But the Behemoth felt no fatigue, and Avotu was alive. Radioactive ash clogged the great warrior's lungs; his muscles burned with fire after days of wandering and soul agony. With each parry, the Blade of Nature lowered more slowly. The golden sparks in the black flame began to dim, absorbed by the absolute pressure of the Abyss.

The monster felt its victim's weakness. The Behemoth stepped back a pace, its giant chest swelling, drawing in the surrounding air, ash, and remnants of mana. The creature was preparing to unleash a wave of pure, concentrated entropy—an attack that did not just kill the body but erased the very essence of matter.
Avotu understood this. He knew that dodging was impossible—behind his back stood hundreds of defenseless lives, Lord Valtor, Kirah, and Yashil.

The great elf drove the Blade of Nature heavily into the stone slabs of the road, knelt on one knee, and raised both hands, concentrating all the remnants of his life force to create an absolute barrier. He prepared to die so that they might live. He had finally found the beautiful death he had sought for so long.
But time in fading worlds flows differently. When the fabric of reality disintegrates, seconds stretch into a thick, transparent resin.
Yashil saw the Behemoth opening its maw. He saw Avotu's shoulders hunching under an invisible weight.

In the little goblin's head, there was neither fear nor doubt. He had no magic to stop this blow. He had no armor to survive it. He had only his life—a light, tiny, green life that this world had always considered worthless filth.
Yashil dropped his rusty pipe.
He rushed forward. His bare feet glided soundlessly over the acid- and ash-soaked stone. He ran past the terror-frozen Dzhanavar, past Rene who had lowered her daggers.
Avotu, concentrated on creating the shield, did not notice the tiny figure darting toward him from the side.
The small, calloused hands of the goblin, with an incredible, desperate strength impossible for his body, pushed the plate-clad elf in the chest.
The strike was unexpected. Avotu lost his balance and fell onto his back, flying several paces to the side. The barrier he had tried to erect burst with a quiet chime, never having formed.
The elf snapped his head up, and his empty eyes widened in primal horror.

Where he himself had stood a second ago, the figure of Yashil now rose. The goblin did not cover his face. He stood straight, squaring his narrow shoulders, and looked directly into the mouth of the encroaching death.
"To give... is not the lot of fools," Yashil whispered, almost inaudibly, in the ringing silence.

A wave of black, concentrated entropy burst from the Behemoth's mouth and, with a deafening, nauseating roar, collapsed upon the spot where the goblin stood. The stone of the Great Road instantly turned to dust, forming a deep, smoking crater.
The shockwave scattered everyone who was nearby.
When the blinding flash of decomposition faded, a dead, vacuum silence hung over the battlefield. It was more terrible than any screams.

Avotu, coughing blood, pulled himself up on his elbows with difficulty. The Behemoth of the Void, having exhausted its energy supply on this colossal strike, slumped heavily, slowly disintegrating into pieces of rotting ash—its body could not withstand its own power.
But the elf did not look at the fallen enemy.

He looked at the edge of the smoking crater. There, on the charred stones, lay a small, motionless green body. The burlap that served as his clothing had decayed. His already fragile bones were hopelessly broken.
From under the crumbling wreckage of elven carts, a sound was heard. It was not a battle cry. It was not the roar of a beast. It was the sound with which a soul is torn to pieces.

Kirah, heavily dragging a wounded limb, crawled out from under the rubble. The huge demon saw the green blood mixed with gray ash and fell to his knees. His burning ruby eyes went out, flooded with impossible, stinging moisture.

The wind over Agdash died down. The leaden skies froze, as if the universe itself held its breath, leaning over the little goblin who had just shown the entire fading world what it means to be truly great.
